Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.

Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

Level 2: you have used 82 actions; a strong score needs about 42 or fewer. You are 2.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check the block positions and blob size now.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] not in ['B', 'W', 'c', 'G']:
        center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']),
                  sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']))
        if center[0] > 5:
            print(f"Node: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, center~({center[0]:.0f},{center[1]:.0f})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The blob's pixel count stayed at 65 (didn't change from previous), but its center moved from (27, 32) to (26, 32). The block moved from (26, 30) to (24, 30).
Wait, the blob didn't shrink this time. Let me reconsider. The blob shrunk from 69 to 65 when the block was at (26, 30). Now the block moved to (24, 30) and the blob stayed at 65.
Maybe the blob only shrinks when a block is at a specific distance from it, not when it's too close. Or maybe the blob shrinks once per block when it first gets close.
Actually, looking at the blob's center movement: (27, 33) -> (27, 32) -> (26, 32). The blob seems to be moving away from the block. This could be the blob "running away" from the blocks.
Let me try a different approach: instead of moving one block very close to the blob, let me try moving multiple blocks towards the blob simultaneously. Maybe the goal is to surround the blob with blocks.
Actually, let me first check if the blob continues to shrink as I move the block closer. Let me move the block at (24, 30) to (25, 31), which is very close to the blob at (26, 32).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move block at (24, 30) closer to blob at (26, 32): click at (25, 31)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 25, 'col': 31}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
